coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen−doped−CQDs/schwertmannites as visible−light−responsive Fenton catalysts for the degradation of chlortetracycline and related cytotoxicity
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yu-Hang
Liu, Xiang-Ji
Xiang, Ji-Zun
Zhang, Zhi-Qiang
Qiao, Xing-Xing
Li, Ya-Feng
He, Qiu-Xiang
Lü, Jian
coredata.dc:description: The heterogeneous photo−Fenton method is a promising technology for wastewater treatment based on the development of efficient catalysts through which the inefficient Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycles and low utilization of H2O2 are optimized. In this work, viable heterogeneous photo−Fenton catalysts (NCS) are synthesized by in situ compounding nitrogen−doped carbon quantum dots (N−CQDs) with schwertmannite (SCH). Specifically, the NCS catalysts exhibit greatly enhanced capacity for chlortetracycline (CTC) degradation, in comparison with those of the pristine SCH and N−CQDs, with a remarkable efficiency of 95.12% at a wide pH range (3.0–7.0). In particular, this allows an efficient Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion due to the fast electron transport capability provided by N−CQDs, coupled to an excellent structural stability of the catalysts. Main active species including •OH, •O2 − and 1O2 are identified in the degradation process, and a possible degradation mechanism is tentatively proposed. In addition, related cytotoxicity of CTC degradation intermediates is verified via co−culturing with E. coli. This study provides a new strategy to overcome the disadvantages of photo−Fenton processes and develop new technologies for advanced water treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Schwertmannite
Carbon quantum dot
Photo−Fenton
Chlortetracycline
Catalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of CTC degradation products and pathways
Identification of intermediates and proposed degradation pathways

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of SCH (a) and NCS−4 (b); (c) TEM images of NCS−4 (Inset: HRTEM of NCS−4); and (d) EDS mapping of Fe, O, C, S and N elements in NCS−4.
(a) XRD of SCH and NCS; (b) FTIR of SCH and NCS; (c) XPS survey spectra and high−resolution XPS spectrum of Fe 2p (d), O 1s (e), C 1s (f), S 2p (g) of SCH and NCS−4.

(a) The CTC degradation efficiency of various catalysts; (b) the CTC degradation efficiency of NCS−4 under different conditions; (c) the CTC degradation efficiency of N−CQDs under different conditions; and (d) the TOC removal rate of CTC. Reaction conditions: 0.5 g L−1 catalyst, 50 mg L−1 CTC solution, and 10 mM H2O2.

The CTC degradation efficiency of NCS−4 at (a) various H2O2 concentrations, (b) catalyst dosage, (c) initial CTC concentration, (d) pH, and (e) different ions; and (f) the cyclic performance of NCS−4.
(a) The Fe(II) concentration and Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio in SCH and NCS−4; (b) EIS spectra of NCS−4 and SCH; (c) PL spectra of NCS−4 and SCH; and (d) cyclic voltammetry curves of SCH and NCS−4.
(a) ESR spectra of DMPO/MeOH−•O2 −, TEMP−1O2, DMPO/H2O−•OH and TEMPO−h+ with NCS−4; and (b) the CTC degradation efficiency of NCS−4 with various scavengers.
(a) The CTC degradation efficiency of NCS−4 and corresponding inhibition rate of E. coli growth; and (b) growth curves of E. coli co−cultured with different solutions.
A proposed mechanism for catalytic CTC degradation with NCS−4 in the photo−Fenton system.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen−doped−CQDs/schwertmannites as visible−light−responsive Fenton catalysts for the degradation of chlortetracycline and related cytotoxicity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, No. 2 Xue Yuan Road, Fuzhou, 350116, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Soil Environmental Health and Regulation, College of Resources and Environment, Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University, No. 15 Shang Xia Dian Road, Fuzhou, 350002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Soil Environmental Health and Regulation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The heterogeneous photo−Fenton method is a promising technology for wastewater treatment based on the development of efficient catalysts through which the inefficient Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycles and low utilization of H2O2 are optimized. In this work, viable heterogeneous photo−Fenton catalysts (NCS) are synthesized by in situ compounding nitrogen−doped carbon quantum dots (N−CQDs) with schwertmannite (SCH). Specifically, the NCS catalysts exhibit greatly enhanced capacity for chlortetracycline (CTC) degradation, in comparison with those of the pristine SCH and N−CQDs, with a remarkable efficiency of 95.12% at a wide pH range (3.0–7.0). In particular, this allows an efficient Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion due to the fast electron transport capability provided by N−CQDs, coupled to an excellent structural stability of the catalysts. Main active species including •OH, •O2 − and 1O2 are identified in the degradation process, and a possible degradation mechanism is tentatively proposed. In addition, related cytotoxicity of CTC degradation intermediates is verified via co−culturing with E. coli. This study provides a new strategy to overcome the disadvantages of photo−Fenton processes and develop new technologies for advanced water treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive use of synthetic chemicals related to the unparallel development of industrialization leads to the accumulation of emerging contaminants to the environment, including persistent organic pollutants, endocrine disruptors, antibiotics and microplastics (Duan et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015). The presence of such emerging contaminants in wastewater has aroused growing concerns worldwide. Among them, antibiotics are characterized by biological toxicity, environmental persistence and bioaccumulation, which lead to potential risks to ecosystems and human health even at low−level exposures (Zhao et al., 2021). Chlortetracycline (CTC), as one of the most typical representatives of antibiotics, has been widely used in medical and veterinary treatments (Bengtsson−Palme et al., 2019; Daghrir et al., 2013; Ellis, 2006; Hamscher et al., 2002). In addition, the accumulation of antibiotics in the environment and organisms would induce microorganisms to develop drug resistance and generate drug resistance genes, further posing threats to the health safety (Boxall et al., 2003; Halling−Sorensen et al., 1998; Schmitt et al., 2006).

In quest of technical solutions to antibiotic contaminations in aqueous phases, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been demonstrated as effective remediation methods (Chen et al., 2021). Especially, Fenton−like reactions are able to degrade antibiotics in the presence of iron−based catalysts (Gou et al., 2021; Jain et al., 2018), yet the limited pH range, low hydrogen peroxide utilization, and inefficient activation of Fe(III) to Fe(II) regeneration largely deter their further advances (Hou et al., 2017; Munoz et al., 2015; Neyens and Baeyens, 2003). To address these issues, photo−Fenton systems are recently developed to harvest solar energy for more sustainable Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion due to the introduction of photogenerated electrons (Liu et al., 2018). Carbon nanomaterials modified catalysts have become increasing research focuses (Song et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2020), in which Fe/C−based composites can significantly improve photo−Fenton reactions by accelerating electron transfer on iron−based materials through the implanted properties of carbon materials (Cao et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020a). However, the toxicity of some carbon nanomaterials may result in unsuitable prospects for practical applications (He et al., 2018). Therefore, it is urgent to design low toxic Fe/C−based catalyst for viable photo−Fenton systems. To this end, schwertmannites (SCHs), as a class of natural iron–oxyhydroxysulfate minerals, have been tentatively used in catalytic degradation of antibiotics in recent years (Cui et al., 2022; Li et al., 2022a). However, the dominant Fe(III) species in SCHs turn to be an important factor restricting the reaction activity of photo−Fenton processes due to the low efficiency of Fe(III) to Fe(II) conversion (Qiao et al., 2021a). To address this issue, the combinational use of reductive carbon nanomaterials and SCHs (Li et al., 2021) might be an effective way to address these issues.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are receiving current research attention in the fields of nanoelectronics, biomedicine and biosensors due to their environmentally friendly nature, excellent water solubility, conductivity and biocompatibility (Wang and Hu, 2014; Zhang et al., 2020). More recently, CQDs are applied as photocatalysts, taking the advantage of exceptional electron storage capacities, which accelerate the electron transfer towards a more effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes (Feng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022b). For this purpose, doping of elements, such as nitrogen, into CQDs can improve their fluorescence and electron donating ability for enhanced photocatalytic performances (Sun et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019a). Composite of up−conversion carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and goethite (CQDs/α− FeOOH) have shown a tetracycline (TC) degradation efficiency of 3.7−fold of the original α−FeOOH. Moreover, the broad−spectrum response of FeOOH is beneficial for the treatment of various surface charged organic pollutants with excellent catalytic performance and durability in different actual water substrates, as well as the nearly complete elimination of related drug resistance under sunlight irradiation (Huang et al., 2020). Meanwhile, it is possible that some degradation products can become more toxic (Zhong et al., 2022), hence it is necessary to unveil the toxicity of intermediates produced during the degradation of organic pollutants (Cheng et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2022).

In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were selected to hybridize with a secondary mineral, schwertmannite (SCH), based on the consideration of their occurrence and abundance in the environment (Hochella et al., 2019), as well as the synergic effect between optically active CQDs and Fenton catalytic activity of SCH. In order to accelerate the Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycles in photo−Fenton reactions, we propose a strategy to construct composite photocatalysts through the combinational use of SCH and N−CQDs for the formation of N−CQDs−loaded SCH (NCS). The effects of various factors on the efficiency of CTC degradation were systematically evaluated. By detecting the free radicals and analysing reaction intermediates in photo−Fenton processes, a degradation mechanism was tentatively proposed. In addition, the toxicity of degradation intermediates was evaluated by bacteriostasis experiments. This study demonstrated that NCS might serve as green and sustainable catalysts for the treatment of aqueous antibiotic contamination in photo−Fenton systems.

Iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4•7H2O), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7•H2O), urea (CH4N2O) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Chlortetracycline (C22H23ClN2O8•HCl), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), tert–butrl alcohol (TBA) and ammonium oxalate (AO) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). 2,2,6,6–tetramethylpiperidine–1–oxyl (TEMPO) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). E. coli (CCTCC AB 93154) was obtained from China Center for Type Culture Collection. All agents used without further purification. UPT−I−5T ultrapure water system was used for the preparation of all solutions and suspensions.

The structural properties of catalysts were tested by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high−resolution microscopy (HRTEM), energy dispersive X−ray spectroscopy (EDS), thermogravimetric (TGA), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP−OES), powder X−ray diffraction (PXRD), X−ray energy spectrum analysis (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), electron spin−resonance spectroscopy (ESR) etc. Details of instrumental characterizations are provided in the Supporting Information.

Nitrogen−dopped carbon quantum dots (N−CQDs) were prepared via a simple method documented in the literature (Wang et al., 2017). For the synthesis of N−CQDs−loaded SCH (NCS), 2.224 g of FeSO4•7H2O and a certain amount of N−CQDs stock solution were added to 98.8 mL deionized water, followed by ultrasonication for 30 min to form a homogeneous dispersion. Afterwards, H2O2 (1.2 mL) was introduced to the above solution by using a syringe pump (0.1 mL min−1) The precipitate was filtrated after 24 h, washed several times with water to a pH of approximately 7.0, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C. A series of NCS nanocomposites with various N−CQDs loadings were prepared via controlling the volume (V) of N−CQDs solutions and denoted as NCS−x (x = 1, V = 10 mL; x = 2, V = 20 mL; x = 3, V = 30 mL; x = 4, V = 40 mL; and x = 5, V = 50 mL). Reference SCH was prepared in a similar process without the addition of N−CQDs solution.

The catalytic activity of N−CQDs, SCH and NCS catalyst was investigated by CTC degradation in a photo−Fenton system in the presence of H2O2. Typically, 15 mg of catalyst was dispersed into a 50 mL quartz vessel containing 30 mL of CTC solution (50 mg L−1), and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 500 rpm in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium, after which 10 mM H2O2 was added and a 300 W Xenon lamp (λ ≥ 400 nm, light intensity = ca. 400 mW/cm2) was applied. The reactor was connected to the circulating water system throughout the experiment to ensure that the temperature was maintained at about 25 °C. Afterwards, 2.0 mL of suspension was extracted, filtered using a 0.22 μm filter tip, and quenched with excessive TBA to remove the ·OH residue. The absorption characteristic at 367 nm was then measured using a UV–vis absorption spectrometry.

The CTC degradation efficiency was calculated as D = C/C 0 × 100%, where C 0 and C are the absorption intensity before and after the reaction, respectively. During each run of a cyclic experiments, the catalyst was thoroughly washed and dried, and then was dispersed in the CTC solution used for a next run of catalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of CTC degradation products and pathways
ce:section.ce:para: CTC degradation intermediates were analysed and determined by an ultra−high performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometer (LC–MS; XevoG2−XSQT of Waters Company) with (A)0.1% formic acid or (B)0.1% formic acid/acetonitrile solution as the eluent at a flow rate of 0.1 mL min−1. The specific gradient elution procedure was set at 95% A (0–5 min), 95−0% A (5–15 min), and immediately declined to 0% A (15–25 min), kept at 95% A (25–30 min). The ion source in the high−resolution mass spectrometry (MS) was an electron ionization (EI) source with a collision energy of 15 V–45 V at a full spectrum scan range of 50 m/z ∼ 1000 m/z.

Escherichia coli was chosen as an experiment due to its high sensitivity to CTC. All experiments were conducted under sterile conditions. Each liquid medium component is a mixed solution of yeast extract, peptone, sodium chloride, CTC and bacteria solution. The medium was cultured in a bacterial incubator at 37 °C, and then 1.0 mL of the solution was extracted into the centrifuge tube hourly. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrometer was used to measure the OD600 to record the growth curve of Escherichia coli. The inhibition rate was calculated with the equation of R = (L0−Lt)/L0 × 100% where L0 and Lt are OD600 of bacteria cultured in sterile water and treated CTC solution, respectively.

Morphologies and microstructures of as−prepared SCH and NCS were observed under SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the SCH displayed microspheres with a mean diameter of about 1.0 μm. Upon the addition of N−CQDs, the particle size of SCH in NCS gradually decreased to diameters between 100 nm and 200 nm (Fig. 1b). The decrease in particle size was attributed to the templating effect of N−CQDs during the growth of SCH. Moreover, the rough surface of NCS was decorated with N−CQDs smaller than 10 nm (Fig. 1c, inset). Elemental mapping clearly indicated the presence of iron (Fe), oxygen (O), carbon (C), sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) in NCS (Fig. 1d).

In the PXRD patterns of NCS (Fig. 2 a), similar distinct peaks at 26.27°, 35.16°, 55.30° and 61.34° could be observed for all samples, which were assigned to SCH (PDF #47–1775) (Duan et al., 2016). However, no obvious diffraction peaks for N−CQDs were detected, possibly due to the high dispersion and low crystallinity of N−CQDs. To investigate surface functionalities of NCS, FT−IR spectra was recorded as shown in Fig. 2b. Characteristics between 3200 cm−1 and 3700 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching mode of –OH and the absorption band near 1637 cm−1 belonged to physically adsorbed water molecules (Gan et al., 2015). In addition, absorption bands at 611 cm−1 and 1068 cm−1 indicated the existence of SO4 2− vibration in SCH and NCS (Zhang et al., 2019b), which increased with increasing N−CQDs contents.

In the Fe 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 2c), characteristics at 712.04 eV and 710.46 eV for SCH and 711.99 eV and 710.56 eV for NCS corresponded to Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively (Ma et al., 2020). The O 1s spectra of SCH and NCS−4 were composed by three peaks of Fe–O–Fe, Fe–O–H and SO4 2− characteristics, of which binding energies of NCS−4 was positively shifted due to Fe–O–C formation upon addition of N−CQDs (Huang et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2020) (Fig. 2d). The C 1s peaks of SCH (Fig. 2e) were observed for CO (288.73 eV), C–O (286.20 eV) and C–C (284.8 eV) (Li et al., 2021). Moreover, the CO characteristic of NCS−4 shifted to 288.43 eV which was attributed to the electronic interaction between SCH and N−CQDs. The S 2p spectra of SCH and NCS−4 displayed two peaks of HSO4 and Fe–SO4 located at 169.82 and 168.62 eV (Fig. 2f), respectively (Qiao et al., 2021b).

The thermal stability of SCH and NCS−4 was studied by thermogravimetric analyses (TGA). According to Fig. S2a, the initial weight losses in the range of 30–250 °C were assigned to the release of absorbed or bound water molecules. The following weight losses between 250–550 °C were attributed to the removal of organic substituents and N−CQDs (Zhu et al., 2013). The final weight loss after 550 °C would arise from the release of SO2 (Qiao et al., 2021a). In addition, the light absorption property of SCH and NCS−4 was measured via UV–vis DRS. As shown in Figs. S1b and 1c, the absorption edges of SCH and NCS−4 at ca. 544 nm and 548 nm indicated sufficient visible light utilization. Meanwhile, the bandgaps (2.24 eV) of SCH and NCS−4 was nearly identical, which was attributable to the small size of N−CQDs with multiple absorption peaks at ultraviolet region caused by π−π* transition (Fig. S1d).

The NCS catalysts generally showed improved efficiency for CTC degradation than that of the pristine SCH (Fig. 3 a). Moreover, the efficiency of CTC degradation gradually increased with increasing N−CQDs loadings. The enhanced CTC degradation efficiency was attributed to the proton release from CQDs under illumination to neutralize OH− produced by H2O2 decomposition (Zhang et al., 2020). However, excessive N−CQDs loading in NCS−5 was unable to further increase the photo−Fenton activity, which was ascribed to the competitive absorption of photons caused by a high content of N−CQDs at the surface of SCH (Li et al., 2022b). Specifically, the rate constant (k) achieved 0.10665 min−1 for NCS−4 (Fig. S1), which was the highest among all samples. Therefore, the NCS−4 was invested as an optimal representative for the degradation of CTC hereafter.

Photo−Fenton degradation of CTC was evaluated with various catalyst (Fig. 3a). Of note, the NCS−4 exhibited the highest efficiency of 95.12% in 60 min in comparison with the pristine N−CQDs (36.79%), SCH (70.2%) and other NCS (90.95%–94.37%). Notably, the CTC removal in a sole photocatalytic (blue) or Fenton (black) system was considerably lower than that of the photo−Fenton system, as demonstrated by NCS−4 (Fig. 3b) and N−CQDs (Fig. 3c), which suggested synergistic effect between photocatalytic and Fenton reactions in this current system. Moreover, the TOC removal rate of NCS−4 (61.47%) was considerably higher than that of the SCH (13.68%), although moderate CTC degradation was achieved. To further optimize the CTC degradation in photo−Fenton systems, control experiments using NCS−4 were performed under various conditions (all experimental conditions were constant at 0.5 g L−1 catalyst, 50 mg L−1 CTC solution, 10 mM H2O2, and pH = 4.1, otherwise stated). As shown in Fig. 4 a, insufficient H2O2 (1.0 mM) resulted in a low degradation rate of ca. 76.5%, and thus an appropriate increase of H2O2 concentration was beneficial to improving the CTC degradation. Unlikely, the removal rate of CTC remained unchanged with various catalyst dosages in photo−Fenton processes (Fig. 4b). Moreover, the degradation efficiency was able to maintain a high level (over 90%) in the initial CTC concentration range of 20–100 mg L−1 (Fig. 4c). In general, traditional Fenton processes suffer from the restricted pH (around 3.0) that deters the technology from practical applications, therefore the pH is studied as a rate−limiting factor in current photo−Fenton systems. Surprisingly, the CTC degradation efficiency of NCS−4 remained essentially constant at a wide pH range from 2.9 to 7.3 (Fig. 4d), which is superior to most heterogeneous Fenton catalysts reported hitherto (Liu et al., 2019; Qiao et al., 2021a). In addition, the effect of interfering cations and anions on CTC degradation were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4e, both anions (SO4 2−, Cl−) and cations (Cu2+, Cd2+) hardly affected the CTC degradation. The stability and reusability of NCS−4 were also studied by successive CTC degradation. It was observed that the NCS−4 could be reused with high catalytic activity (over 90%) for more than 5 cycles (Fig. 4f). Furthermore, PXRD patterns of NCS−4 before and after cyclic reactions were similar with each other (Fig. S3), indicating that the excellent reusability of the catalyst.

To investigate the Fe(III) to Fe(II) conversion in this system, the concentration of ferrous irons in reaction solutions was monitored (Fig. S4a). Interestingly, the Fe(II) concentration increased in the first 30 min and then decreased thereafter for the examined systems. This was mainly attributable to the presence of N−CQDs that effectively accelerated the migration of photoelectrons, which greatly accelerated Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion for the activation of H2O2 to initiate heterogeneous photo−Fenton processes. However, CTC was gradually degraded and resulted in excessive •OH that might oxidize Fe(II) to Fe(III). By contrast, both the concentration of Fe(II) and Fe(II):Fe(III) ratio in SCH and NCS−4 were significantly increased with prolonging photo−Fenton reactions (Fig. 5 a). This is a key evidence for the Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion promoted by photoelectrons at the surface of photocatalysts.

To unveil the excellent photo−Fenton performance of NCS−4 for CTC removal, photoelectrochemical characterizations were performed. As shown in Fig. S3b, the transient photocurrent responsese of NCS−4 under light was significantly higher than that of SCH, and the NCS−4 exhibited the smallest charge transfer resistance in electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS; Fig. 5b). Moreover, the weaker fluorescence signal of NCS−4 relative to CNNS was reflected in the PL spectra (Fig. 5c), indicating the lower electron−hole pair complexation rate of NCS−4.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was recorded to investigate the redox potential and electron transfer in SCH and NCS−4 (Fig. 5d). The presence of two weak redox peaks in the CV of SCH indicated that the weaker redox reaction limited Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion (Li et al., 2021). In contrast, two very strong redox peaks were observed for the NCS−4 and the significant enhancement in current intensity, suggesting a faster electron transfer and stronger redox reaction at the surface of NCS−4 (Zhu et al., 2020b). Therefore, the introduction of N−CQDs in SCH could accelerate electron transfer and thus promoted Fe(III) to Fe(II) conversion to enhance the performance of photo−Fenton systems.
A possible catalytic mechanism of NCS−4 was proposed as follows (Scheme 1 .): photocarriers.

(electron−hole pairs) were generated under visible irradiation (Wang et al., 2022), and then photoelectrons were rapidly transferred to the catalyst surface through N−CQDs, which initiated the conversion of surface Fe(III) into Fe(II); following Fenton reactions involving Fe(II) and H2O2 generated •OH, together with other photogenerated reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Shi et al., 2014), to participate in CTC degradation. Reactions are as follows:
(1) NCS−4 + hʋ → h+ + e−
(2) N−CQDs + e− → N−CQDs (e−)
(3) N−CQDs (e−) + Fe(III) → N−CQDs + Fe (II)
(4) Fe(III) + H2O2 → Fe(II) + H+ + •O2H
(5) Fe(II) + H2O2 → •OH + Fe(III) + OH−
(6) O2 + N−CQDs(e−) → •O2 − + N−CQDs
(7) •O2 − + •OH → 1O2 + HO−
(8) •O2 − + •OH + 1O2 + CTC → H2O + CO2 + intermediate products

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of intermediates and proposed degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: LC−MS revealed the possible degradation pathway of CTC during the photo−Fenton process. The peak with a retention time around 14.0 min was assigned to pristine CTC molecules, which decreased significantly during reactions, indicating the effective degradation of CTC (Fig. S5). Approximately ten intermediates were identified, and three CTC degradation pathways were proposed (see Fig. 7 ). In pathway 1, the CTC was first dechlorinated to form compound P1 (m/z = 444). Subsequently, product P2 (m/z = 362) was produced from the removal of amide and dimethylamino. The product P3 (m/z = 318) was generated due to demethylation. Afterwards, the product P4 (m/z = 274) was generated via dehydroxylation. In pathway 2, the CTC molecule was degraded to compound P5 (m/z = 451) by a demethylation reaction and was subsequently converted to P6 (m/z = 433) by a dehydration reaction. The product P7 (m/z = 355) was further generated by dechlorination and deamidation reactions. Finally, P10 (m/z = 280) was generated by deamidation, demethylation and dehydroxylation. In pathway 3, P5 could be oxidized by ROS to generate P8 (m/z = 360) and P9 (m/z = 328) via deamidation and dihydroxylation, respectively. Eventually, most of these intermediates were mineralized to inorganic salts, CO2 and H2O (Chen et al., 2022; Fang et al., 2020; Qiao et al., 2021a; Tu et al., 2022).

E. coli was selected as the model bacteria to be co−cultured with various untreated and treated solutions to reveal the toxicity of degradation intermediates. Although the CTC concentration greatly decreased in the first 30 min, it was observed that the inhibition rate of E. coli kept at high levels (Fig. 8 a). Afterwards, the E. coli inhibition rate of treated CTC solutions was considerably decreasing, and the antibacterial effect of treated CTC solutions was insignificant after 50 min. The OD600 of co−cultured pristine CTC solution (untreated) remained constantly at a very low level within 12h (Fig. 8b), showing significant inhibition of E. coli growth. By contrast, sterile water (CK) and CTC solution photodegraded for 60 min (treated) barely affected the growth of E. coli, indicating a low toxicity of degradation products after 60 min.

A comparison of antibiotics removal efficiency and cytotoxicity of intermediates with various catalysts is shown in Table S2. It is clear that some catalysts are effective in the degradation of original antibiotics, however, their degradation products remain harmful due to the conversion into toxic intermediates (Yang et al., 2014; Chen and Huang, 2010). In contrast, the heterogeneous Fenton or photocatalytic reaction is more likely to yield less toxic or even non−toxic degradation products (Usman et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020). This may originate from the generation of free radicals or non−radical species which attack the π−conjugating aromatic systems in the contaminant molecules, rather than undergoing simple redox reactions towards the decorating subunits. Furthermore, the NCS−4 in this study shows considerable advantages including the involvement of inexpensive raw materials, as well as the achievement of a high CTC degradation efficiency at relatively low dosage.

A class of new heterogeneous Fenton catalysts (NCS) were successfully prepared nitrogen−doped carbon quantum dots (N−CQDs) and schwertmannite (SCH). Compared with the original N−CQDs and SCH, the optimal NCS−4 has significantly enhanced the degradation ability of CTC hydrochloride and achieved 95.12% degradation rate at 60 min in the photo−Fenton system. This is mainly because N−CQDs in the composite catalysts can accelerate the electron transfer at the interface, which promotes the cycle of Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion in photo−Fenton reactions. In addition, the composite catalysts also showed a wide applicable pH range and excellent cycling performances. Furthermore, radical trapping tests show that •OH, •O2− and 1O2 contribute significantly to the degradation of CTC. More importantly, it is verified that CTC solution after photodegradation is nearly harmless to E. coli. Therefore, this photo−Fenton system is promising for applications in the removal of antibiotic contaminants in wastewater from breeding and pharmaceutical industry. Further application of these composite photo−Fenton catalysts in the membrane technology might facilitate the treatment of antibiotic wastewater in a catalytic reactor for a more sustainable photo−Fenton system.

Yu-Hang Xu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xiang–Ji Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Ji–Zun Xiang: Investigation, Methodology. Zhi–Qiang Zhang: Methodology. Xing-Xing Qiao: Supervision, Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Ya-Feng Li: Writing – review & editing. Qiu-Xiang He: Formal analysis. Jian Lü: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the Open Project Program of the State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment (Grant No. SKLPEE–202008), Fuzhou University, and the Special Fund for Scientific and Technological Innovation of Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University (Grant No. CXZX2019073G).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
